food_subgroup: Steamed glutinous rice dumplings with filled chocolate and cream sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn class for mochi-skin chocolate-filled xiao long bao. Mapped to 'dumpling food product' (FOODON_03315091) as the closest defensible ancestor class. The mochi skin differentiates this from standard wheat-dough dumplings.
ontology.foodex2_description: Steamed dumplings (dim sum type) — closest FoodEx2 category for steamed filled dumplings in the dim sum family; mochi-skin variant not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for mochi-skin chocolate dumplings. A0FMT (steamed dumplings) used as the closest defensible anchor. Chocolate filling and sea salt cream sauce are not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept for this fusion dessert dumpling product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Mama Mochi Chocolate Xiao Long Bao consists of approximately 4 to 5 pieces with sea salt cream sauce, weighing around 120 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Mama Mochi Chocolate Xiao Long Bao With Sea Salt Cream — HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient record (G00010067)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical record G00010067 for this specific dish. Saturated fat is not separately reported in the HPB record; estimated from analogous mochi-chocolate dessert composition using USDA FoodData Central reference (USDA SR Legacy: 'Candies, chocolate, dark' and 'Mochi, rice cake') as a supplementary estimate. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and water are not reported in the HPB source and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of glutinous rice and glutinous rice-based products — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous rice (GI ~86) and Atkinson FS et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific product. GI estimated at 86 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous rice (GI ~86), which is the primary carbohydrate source in the mochi skin. The chocolate filling and sea salt cream sauce contribute additional sugar but do not substantially lower the GI of the glutinous rice base. Glycemic load per 120 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (86 × 12.43) / 100 ≈ 10.7. Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.94 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre and negligible potassium (5 mg/100 g), creating an unfavourable sugar-to-potassium ratio; sea salt cream sauce adds additional sodium load beyond the 3 mg/100 g base value.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of this high-sugar, zero-fibre dessert; the sea salt cream sauce contributes additional sodium and the absence of potassium provides no counterbalancing vasodilatory benefit, consistent with AHA 2018 guidance on added-sugar restriction for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glutinous rice flour mochi skin has a very high glycaemic index (~86), and sugar accounts for 9.94 g/100 g with zero dietary fibre to blunt postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dessert because the glutinous rice mochi skin combined with chocolate filling and sea salt cream sauce produces a rapid and sustained postprandial glucose spike; if consumed, portion should be restricted to one piece and paired with a high-fibre food to partially attenuate glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added-sugar content (9.94 g/100 g) with zero fibre, zero meaningful micronutrients, and saturated fat from the sea salt cream sauce contribute to an atherogenic dietary pattern when consumed regularly.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should treat this as an occasional indulgence only; the combination of high sugar, saturated fat from cream, and negligible fibre is inconsistent with AHA dietary guidelines for cardiovascular risk reduction, and regular consumption may adversely affect triglyceride and LDL-cholesterol levels.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar (9.94 g/100 g) with zero fibre and zero anti-inflammatory nutrients promotes pro-inflammatory mediator activity; glutinous rice is a high-glycaemic-load refined carbohydrate.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dessert as its high sugar content, zero fibre, and saturated fat from cream are associated with increased systemic inflammation and may exacerbate joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mama Mochi Chocolate Xiao Long Bao with Sea Salt Cream is a Singaporean fusion dessert that reimagines the classic Chinese steamed dumpling (xiao long bao) using a chewy glutinous rice flour (mochi) skin in place of the traditional wheat-dough wrapper. Each piece is filled with rich, premium dark chocolate that melts and oozes upon biting, mimicking the soup-burst experience of a savoury xiao long bao. The dumplings are served with a house-made sea salt cream sauce that provides a sweet-salty contrast to the chocolate interior. The texture is characteristically soft, sticky, and chewy from the glutinous rice skin, with a molten chocolate centre and a lightly salted cream finish. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-sparse at 42 kcal per 100 g but is dominated by simple sugars (9.94 g/100 g) derived from the chocolate filling and cream sauce, with negligible protein (0 g/100 g, confirmed zero per HPB record), very low fat (0.25 g/100 g), and zero dietary fibre. The glutinous rice mochi skin carries a very high glycaemic index (~86), making this dessert clinically unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without strict portion control. It is best regarded as an occasional indulgence. The dish is a signature item in Singapore's modern dim sum dessert scene, blending Japanese mochi technique with Chinese xiao long bao presentation and Western chocolate confectionery.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical record G00010067 with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous mochi-chocolate dessert composition. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database glutinous rice entry (GI ~86) as the closest approved analogous source, with estimation basis fully documented.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo record G00010067; this is a carbohydrate-and-fat-dominant dessert product with negligible protein by formulation — confirmed zero per HPB source. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB record; glutinous rice flour and chocolate filling as formulated contain no measurable fibre. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per HPB record. magnesium_mg is confirmed zero per HPB record (0.0 mg/100 g reported). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific Singapore restaurant dessert product are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. saturated_fat_g (0.12 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for dark chocolate and mochi rice cake as the closest approved analogous sources; the HPB record does not separately report saturated fat. GI of 86 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database glutinous rice entry; no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists in any approved source. Halal status not confirmed; dish contains dairy (cream) and chocolate (possible soy lecithin); allergen list is indicative. The food_subgroup has been updated from 'Batter-based dishes' to 'Steamed glutinous rice dumplings with filled chocolate and cream sauce' to more accurately reflect the product. food_group updated from 'Mixed' to 'Desserts' based on product nature.
